title: Characteristics of Each Method
narrationScript: Type and method are the two axes of every penetrant system. Type tells you how the indication shows itself, fluorescent under ultraviolet light or visible red dye under white light. Method tells you how the excess penetrant comes off the surface, and that is what this lesson is about, because the removal step is where sensitivity is won or lost. Once penetrant has soaked into a flaw, everything that follows is an exercise in taking penetrant off the surface without taking it out of the flaw. Method A is water washable. The penetrant has an emulsifying agent built in, so a plain water spray rinses it away. One material, one step, fast and cheap, and it rinses cleanly out of threads, keyways, and rough as cast surfaces that you could never wipe by hand. That convenience carries a built in risk. Because the penetrant in the flaw is just as washable as the penetrant on the surface, an aggressive wash strips the flaw too. Typical procedure values keep the rinse to a coarse spray at no more than about forty pounds per square inch, with water between roughly fifty and one hundred degrees Fahrenheit, and you stop the moment the background is gone. More pressure, hotter water, or a few extra passes for a nice clean look is how shallow flaws disappear. Over washing is the classic penetrant sin. And if you suspect it happened, there is no touch up. You clean the part, dry it, and start again from the penetrant step. Methods B and D split the job into two materials. The penetrant itself is not water washable. It stays put until you apply a separate emulsifier or remover that makes the surface layer rinsable. Method B uses a lipophilic emulsifier, an oil based material applied by dipping the part or flowing it over the surface. It works by diffusing into the penetrant layer, which makes contact time the critical variable. Leave it on too long and it diffuses down into the flaws, and the rinse washes them out. Typical contact times run from tens of seconds to a few minutes, the part is not agitated while the emulsifier works, and the water rinse stops the action. Method D uses a hydrophilic remover, a water based detergent. The sequence runs differently. First a plain water pre rinse knocks off the bulk of the surface penetrant. Then the part goes into the remover bath, typically somewhere between five and thirty five percent concentration for a dip, and much weaker if sprayed, commonly five percent or less. Then a final rinse. The detergent works by scrubbing penetrant from the surface, and only the surface. It does not diffuse down into the flaw the way a lipophilic emulsifier can. That is why hydrophilic processing is the controlled choice for the highest sensitivity work, and why its bath concentration gets checked with a refractometer as part of routine process control. Method C is solvent removable, the portable method. No tanks, no water. You wipe the excess off with a clean, dry, lint free cloth until most of it is gone, then finish with a cloth lightly dampened with solvent remover. The rule that protects you is simple. Never flood the surface. Solvent sprayed or poured onto the part runs straight into every flaw and dissolves the penetrant out instantly. Done right, on a smooth machined surface with a nonaqueous developer, solvent removable work is highly sensitive. It is also completely dependent on the hands doing the wiping, and it only makes economic sense on small areas. A word on sensitivity. Fluorescent systems are graded into sensitivity levels, from level one half at the bottom through level four at the top. Higher levels find finer flaws, and they also light up more background on rough surfaces. Visible dye systems are not graded into those levels and sit at the low end of the scale. Sensitivity is a property of the whole system, penetrant family, removal method, developer, and your technique on the day. So which method is best? Wrong question. The honest comparison is what each one costs and what each one demands. Water washable is the cheapest to run and the easiest to ruin. Post emulsifiable buys back sensitivity at the price of an extra material, extra stations, and real process control, timers for contact, refractometer checks for concentration. Solvent removable trades equipment for labor and skill. Pick the one your procedure calls for, run it inside its limits, and verify it every day against a known defect panel. The method you can actually control beats the fancier one you cannot.
imagePrompt: Gloved hands rinse a machined steel component with a coarse low-pressure water spray at a penetrant wash station, fluorescent green penetrant sheeting off the surface under ultraviolet light in a darkened booth.

sections.heading: One Goal, Four Removal Strategies
sections.body:
Every penetrant system is classified on two independent axes. **Type** is how the indication shows itself: Type I fluoresces yellow-green under UV-A; Type II is a red dye seen under white light. **Method** is how the excess surface penetrant comes off: **A** water-washable, **B** post-emulsifiable lipophilic, **C** solvent-removable, **D** post-emulsifiable hydrophilic. The axes are separate decisions - a fluorescent penetrant can be water-washable or post-emulsifiable; a visible penetrant is most often solvent-removable in the field.

This lesson is about the method axis, because removal is where a penetrant test is won or lost. After the dwell, penetrant sits in two places: a film on the surface and an entrapment inside any surface-breaking flaw. The entire removal problem is taking off the first without disturbing the second. Each method solves that problem with a different chemistry, and each chemistry brings its own sensitivity ceiling, cost structure, failure mode, and process-control burden. By the end of this lesson you should be able to look at any of the four process flows and say what it does well, what it risks, and what you must control to run it honestly.

sections.caption: Process flow for each method between penetrant dwell and developer - the amber step is the removal control that sets sensitivity.

sections.heading: Method A: Water-Washable
sections.body:
Method A penetrant carries a built-in emulsifying agent, so plain water rinses it off. One material, one removal step. The typical procedure values: a **coarse spray**, water pressure commonly **no more than about 40 psi**, water temperature roughly **50–100 °F**, and the wash stops **the moment background is gone** - judged under UV-A for Type I or adequate white light for Type II, watching the surface, not the clock.

The strengths follow from simplicity: lowest material and labor cost per part at volume, fast cycle times, and clean rinsing out of **threads, keyways, drilled holes, and rough as-cast or forged surfaces** that no one could wipe by hand. That is why high-volume casting and production lines run Method A.

The weakness is built into the same chemistry: penetrant *inside the flaw* is exactly as water-tolerant as penetrant on the surface. Raise the pressure, warm the water, linger for a cosmetically perfect background, or hold the nozzle too close, and you strip the flaw entrapment - **over-washing**, the classic PT sin, and shallow or broad flaws go first. There is no touch-up: if you suspect over-wash, the only honest recovery is **re-clean, dry, and reprocess from the penetrant step**. The opposite error is just as real - penetrant left to dry on the part during an overlong dwell becomes nearly impossible to remove correctly.

sections.heading: Methods B and D: Post-Emulsifiable, Two Chemistries
sections.body:
Post-emulsifiable penetrants are **not water-washable by themselves** - that is the point. The flaw entrapment stays protected until a separate material makes the *surface* layer rinsable. Two chemistries do that job.

**Method B - lipophilic.** The emulsifier is oil-based, applied by **immersion or flowing**, never scrubbed or agitated, because it works by **diffusing into the penetrant layer on a timed contact**. Contact time is the critical variable - commonly tens of seconds to a few minutes, set by the procedure and verified by timer. Too short and the surface will not rinse clean; too long and the emulsifier diffuses into flaw entrapments, which then wash away. The water rinse stops the action.

**Method D - hydrophilic.** The remover is a water-based detergent, and the sequence differs: a plain **water pre-rinse** first removes the bulk of the surface penetrant (and extends remover-bath life), then the part enters the remover - **immersion typically at about 5–35% concentration; spray application far weaker, commonly 5% or less** - then a final rinse. The detergent **acts only at the surface**, scrubbing penetrant off rather than diffusing into it, so flaw entrapments survive even with some margin in contact time. That controllability is why hydrophilic processing dominates **high-sensitivity aerospace work**, and why bath concentration is verified with a **refractometer** as routine process control.

Both methods buy sensitivity - especially for **shallow, broad flaws** - at the price of extra materials, extra stations, and real timing discipline.

sections.caption: The same crack under three removal techniques: a controlled wash and hydrophilic removal leave the entrapment intact, while over-washing flushes it out.

sections.heading: Method C: Solvent-Removable
sections.body:
Method C trades the tank line for a wipe. Removal is by hand: first a **clean, dry, lint-free cloth** takes off the bulk of the excess; then a cloth **lightly dampened with solvent remover** finishes the surface. The one inviolable rule: **never flood, spray, or pour solvent onto the examination surface** - solvent reaching a flaw dissolves the entrapment instantly, and the test dies right there.

Method C is the **portable** method: no water, no electricity, no fixed stations. With Type II visible penetrant it is the standard field kit - cleaner/remover, red penetrant, and nonaqueous developer in aerosol cans - for welds, maintenance checks, and one-off examinations in place. With Type I fluorescent penetrant it serves small-area, high-sensitivity checks in the shop. Paired with a **nonaqueous wet developer** - the most sensitive developer form in common spot use - a careful solvent-removable examination on a smooth machined surface is highly sensitive.

The trade-offs: it is slow per unit area, economical only on **small areas**, and more dependent on operator technique than any other method. The cloth is the process - and so is the restraint of the hand holding it.

sections.caption: Method characteristics at a glance - typical procedure values; your written procedure governs.
rows: Built-in emulsifier; coarse water spray (≤ ~40 psi, ~50–100 °F)
Good general sensitivity; shallow/broad flaws vulnerable
Wash pressure, temperature, time - stop at background
Oil-based emulsifier diffuses on timed contact, then rinse
High; protects shallow flaws when contact is timed right
High on smooth surfaces and small areas
Minimal equipment; high labor per area
Operator technique; cloth and solvent housekeeping
Water pre-rinse; detergent remover (dip ~5–35%, spray ≤ ~5%); final rinse

sections.title: Sensitivity is a system property
sections.body: Fluorescent systems are graded into **sensitivity levels ½ (ultralow) through 4 (ultrahigh)**; visible-dye systems are not graded into those levels and sit at the low end of the scale. The level you achieve comes from the whole system - penetrant family, removal method, developer form, and technique. Higher level means finer flaws found **and** more background noise on rough surfaces. Run the level the flaw of concern requires - more is not automatically better.

sections.heading: Cost and Control: Run What You Can Maintain
sections.body:
Comparing methods honestly means comparing what they demand, not just what they promise:

- **Method A** needs disciplined wash parameters - a pressure gauge and thermometer on the rinse line, and trained eyes on background - because its failure mode is silent and irreversible.
- **Method B** needs timing discipline: emulsifier contact verified by timer, no agitation, consistent drain orientation so contact is uniform.
- **Method D** needs measurement: remover concentration by refractometer, contact times, bath temperature, and water-content checks on the penetrant.
- **Method C** needs craft: clean cloths, controlled solvent use, and the self-restraint never to flood.

All four share one non-negotiable: a **daily system performance check** against a known-defect comparator panel before production parts run, proving the whole chain - materials, process, lighting - still finds flaws. The cheapest method run badly is the most expensive thing in the shop, because its real cost is the flaw that ships. Run the method your procedure names, inside its limits, and prove it every day.

checkQuestions.question: What makes Method A uniquely vulnerable to over-washing?
options: Its penetrant is more viscous than other families
The built-in emulsifier makes the flaw entrapment exactly as water-tolerant as the surface film
Water-washable penetrants use a weaker dye
Method A requires hot, high-pressure water by procedure
checkQuestions.explanation: Because the emulsifying agent is blended into the penetrant itself, the penetrant inside a flaw rinses just as readily as the surface excess - so pressure, temperature, or time beyond the procedure strips indications.

checkQuestions.question: Why does hydrophilic (Method D) removal preserve shallow-flaw sensitivity better than an aggressive water wash?
options: The detergent remover acts only on the surface layer rather than diffusing into flaw entrapments
The remover chemically brightens the penetrant held in the flaw
The pre-rinse drives extra penetrant down into the flaw
Method D skips the final rinse, so nothing is removed from the flaw
checkQuestions.explanation: Hydrophilic removers work by detergent action at the surface only; they do not migrate into the entrapment the way water (or an over-timed lipophilic emulsifier) can, which is why Method D is the high-sensitivity choice.

checkQuestions.question: On a lipophilic (Method B) line, which variable most directly decides whether flaw entrapments survive removal?
options: Developer dwell time
Water-spray droplet size
UV-A lamp intensity
Emulsifier contact time
checkQuestions.explanation: Lipophilic emulsifier works by diffusion into the penetrant layer; contact time controls how deep that action reaches. Too long, and it emulsifies the entrapment itself, which the rinse then removes.

checkQuestions.question: The correct Method C removal technique is:
options: Spray solvent directly onto the surface, then wipe once
Wipe with a dry lint-free cloth first, then a solvent-dampened cloth - never flooding the surface
Soak the part briefly in solvent, then air-dry
Wipe with a solvent-saturated rag until the surface looks polished
checkQuestions.explanation: Bulk excess comes off with a clean dry cloth; the residue comes off with a cloth lightly dampened with remover. Solvent applied directly to the surface runs into flaws and dissolves the entrapment.

checkQuestions.question: Your procedure qualifies a Level 2 fluorescent system for rough as-cast housings. The storeroom offers a Level 4 penetrant "because it's more sensitive." The process-correct response is:
options: Use Level 4 - higher sensitivity always improves detection
Use Level 4 but wash longer to knock down the background
Run the Level 2 system the procedure specifies - a higher level mainly adds background noise on rough surfaces and is not what was qualified
Blend the two penetrants to average the sensitivity
checkQuestions.explanation: Sensitivity level is part of the qualified system. On rough surfaces a higher level lights up background that masks indications, and washing longer to compensate invites over-wash - run what the procedure qualified.

references: CP-105-2024 PT Topical Outline §3.2 (characteristics of each method)
ASTM E165/E165M, method descriptions and processing parameters (cited generically)
SAE AMS 2644 classification - types, methods, sensitivity levels, developer forms (cited generically)
